item.title: Angau faces 10,000 annual births amid low contraceptive use
item.description: ANGAU Memorial Hospital’s labour ward is approaching 10,000 deliveries a year, while Morobe’s contraceptive prevalence rate sits at just eight per cent and its fertility rate at 4.05 children per woman, an official says. The figures were presented this week by Dr Kamo Dumo, the senior medical officer and obstetrician -gynaecologist at the hospital during the Morobe health authority review.
item.content:encoded:
ANGAU Memorial Hospital’s labour ward is approaching 10,000 deliveries a year, while Morobe’s contraceptive prevalence rate sits at just eight per cent and its fertility rate at 4.05 children per woman, an official says.

The figures were presented this week by Dr Kamo Dumo, the senior medical officer and obstetrician -gynaecologist at the hospital during the Morobe health authority review.

“We are not even close to the halfway mark,” Dumo said.

Morobe’s population of 1.1 million is growing at three per cent annually, with adolescents making up 58 per cent of the provincial population.

She said that the Angau labour ward was approaching 10,000 deliveries per year – a figure that had climbed steadily since the hospital began keeping delivery records in 2013.

“It is hitting 10,000 already,” Dumo said.

The perinatal mortality rate of 43.8 per 1,000 live births is more than three times the global target of 12.

Dumo called for a new standalone maternal hospital.

“The globally accepted family size is two. Something to think about. You work out the numbers two years on from these statistics.

Authority chief executive officer Dr Kipas Binga said the health workforce must drive community awareness.

“We need to put more effort into making awareness on family planning, and it comes down to us, health workers,” Binga said.

“Are we doing enough to inform and educate our people?

“Choose your partner wisely and decide how many children you want to have.”

Binga said that for an ordinary Papua New Guinean, raising a child from birth through schooling cost between K300,000 and K500,000.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Sisters honour late father’s legacy as duo law graduates
item.description: THEIR father’s dream became a reality yesterday when sisters Marie-Therese and Sharon Kesly Korei graduated with law degrees at the University of Papua New Guinea (UPNG). “It was our late father’s dream to see at least one of his children follow his footsteps in the legal profession,” Marie-Therese said.
item.content:encoded:
THEIR father’s dream became a reality yesterday when sisters Marie-Therese and Sharon Kesly Korei graduated with law degrees at the University of Papua New Guinea (UPNG).

“It was our late father’s dream to see at least one of his children follow his footsteps in the legal profession,” Marie-Therese said.

They were among the 195 law students who graduated from the UPNG law school.

Yesterday was both a time for celebration and a quiet tribute to the man who first envisioned their path into law school.

As the youngest, Marie-Therese admitted that studying law was not her first choice but their father George insisted that she applied to law school after Grade 12.

In 2024, they lost their father to cancer.

“Losing him was very hard on me. I was used to his guidance all my life and when he passed, I gave up on school and nearly dropped out,” she said,

But her family, particularly her uncles and close friends, stood beside her.

And older sister Sharon was her guide and disciplinarian.

“Sharon has always been there for me, we encourage each other and support each other academically and financially,” Marie-Therese said.

After finishing Grade 12 in 2015, Sharon failed to secure a place in any university.

So she spent seven years working as a sales rep and cashier.

She also upgraded her marks through flexible learning programmes while saving money for fees and books.

“In 2021, I applied as a non-school leaver and got selected to study law,” Sharon said.

Sharon too wanted to become a doctor. But her father’s influence made her change course too.

Marie-Therese plans to help people resolve conflicts and better understand their rights.

“I want to advocate for peace in our community and inform people about their basic human rights,” she said.

Yesterday, the sisters sorely missed their father.

“If he was here, he would be proud. But at the same time, he would also have very high expectations of us,” Marie-Therese said.

Sharon added: “He would be running after us up to the podium. He would be so proud and happy.”

The Korei sisters hail from Madang and East Sepik but were born and raised in Vanimo, West Sepik.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: BSP announces K278 million profit for first quarter
item.description: THE Bank South Pacific Financial Limited Group (BSP) has declared an unaudited net profit of K278 million in the first quarter of 2026 – up 14.6 per cent from the same period last year. Chief executive officer Mark Robinson said this reflected continued operational discipline and the strength of the bank’s diversified earnings base.
item.content:encoded:
THE Bank South Pacific Financial Limited Group (BSP) has declared an unaudited net profit of K278 million in the first quarter of 2026 – up 14.6 per cent from the same period last year.

Chief executive officer Mark Robinson said this reflected continued operational discipline and the strength of the bank’s diversified earnings base.

“Our capital position remains strong, with a capital adequacy ratio of 23.9 per cent, well above regulatory requirements,” he said.

“We continue to make good progress, executing our modernising for growth programme.

“As anticipated, these investments have contributed to higher operating expenses during the quarter.”

He said that BSP was approaching “peak expenditure for the programme during 2026 and 2027, after which we expect our cost-to-income ratio to normalise within our 42 per cent to 45 per cent target range”.

The unaudited statutory Net Profit After Tax was K278 million – up 14.6 per cent.

“Total operating expenses increased 22.3 per cent as BSP continues investing in modernising the business to better serve our customers,” he added.

Robinson said with PNG placed on the Financial Action Task Force’s grey list in February, “we have not observed any material impacts on our customers or operations, and do not currently expect significant disruption”.

He said BSP maintained robust compliance, know-you-customer and anti-money laundering frameworks. And it is committed to supporting national efforts to exit the grey list.

Robinson added: “The global environment has become more uncertain, with ongoing conflict in the Middle East contributing to higher oil prices and potential supply chain disruptions. This raises the risk of elevated inflation and softer economic growth globally and across our region.

“In this context, we continue to take a prudent and disciplined approach to risk management. Asset quality remains sound, and portfolio performance is in line with expectations. We are actively monitoring emerging risks across our lending book and assessing whether any additional provisioning may be required. As at March 31, no changes to provisioning assumptions have been applied.

“BSP is committed to supporting the communities in which we operate. Through our continued investment in financial inclusion, improved access to banking services, and community programs, we play an important role in driving economic participation and development across the South Pacific.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Health issues, food shortages hit Morobe’s Huon Gulf
item.description: HUNDREDS of people in Huon Gulf, Morobe, are facing food shortage, clean water and health issues as a result of the devastation caused by Cyclone Maila , according to district administrator Andrew Namuesh. The District Development Authority (DDA) conducted a disaster impact assessment after heavy rain and strong winds hit the district.
item.content:encoded:
HUNDREDS of people in Huon Gulf, Morobe, are facing food shortage, clean water and health issues as a result of the devastation caused by Cyclone Maila , according to district administrator Andrew Namuesh.

The District Development Authority (DDA) conducted a disaster impact assessment after heavy rain and strong winds hit the district.

The assessment report concluded that the Morobe Local Level Government was the worst hit and urgently needed relief assistance in terms of food, clean water and medical kits.

Joyce-Anne Zamunu, from the Morobe team, said the LLG experienced severe flooding resulting in extensive damage to gardens and infrastructure.

Eight schools (elementary, primary and technical), clinics and houses in the Waria Valley were damaged.

“The Gugumi and Waria rivers rerouted, running through the village,” she said.

“Waria swept away half of Kobo village, creating a path near the Kobo Primary School.

The Salamua team reported flash floods, landslides and rising sea levels destroying gardens, houses, classrooms and clean water sources.

Representative Denva Winjan said some schools suspended classes for at least a week.

District Environmental Health officer Ziruru Ososo said the Wampar LLG also recorded widespread destruction.

Namuesh said the DDA had allocated K200,000 for relief assistance.

A disaster emergency report has been submitted to the provincial disaster office and to provincial administrator Max Brutan.

“We have to respond as soon as possible but this now depends on funding availability to boost the district efforts,” Namuesh said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Luai: Not for money
item.description: SAMOAN international Jarome Luai has broken his silence on his landmark decision to join the PNG Chiefs, insisting the move is driven by purpose rather than profit. Speaking to Australian media, Luai dismissed speculation that tax-free earnings were the lure.
item.content:encoded:
SAMOAN international Jarome Luai has broken his silence on his landmark decision to join the PNG Chiefs, insisting the move is driven by purpose rather than profit.

Speaking to Australian media, Luai dismissed speculation that tax-free earnings were the lure.

“I think everyone wants me to say it’s tax-free and how appealing that is, but that’s not what got the deal signed,” he said. Instead, Luai pointed to a deeper mission. His recent visit to PNG reshaped his perspective: “Being over there, you realise it’s bigger than a game. When the time comes, we’ll be able to change a lot of lives for the better.”

A family trip to Port Moresby proved pivotal. Sharing the experience with his young son, Luai said: “After that visit, that’s probably what got us here.” The environment and vision behind the new franchise resonated strongly with him.

Cultural ties also played a role. “Being Polynesian, there are a lot of similarities with Samoa,” Luai explained. “What that team is going to do for those people … it’s about growing a nation. That’s something that really resonated with me.”

The Wests Tigers playmaker described the recruitment process as swift but transparent.

“It happened pretty quickly. It was a whirlwind, to be honest. We broke no rules throughout the process. The club and coach were very transparent. It was a smooth process.”

He has already expressed gratitude to his current teammates and staff for their support.

Luai’s signing as the Chiefs’ foundation player is being hailed as historic, yet it has sparked debate across the NRL. While the move is expected to elevate the profile of the 19th franchise, critics warn it could disrupt competition balance and trigger a wave of player movement.

Industry figures argue the Chiefs may enjoy a built-in advantage, with reports suggesting tax-free structures could allow players to earn more than under standard NRL contracts. Rival clubs fear they cannot compete on equal terms.

“This changes the market,” one insider said. “If players can earn significantly more for the same salary, it becomes very hard to keep them.”

Concerns also extend to recruitment. Luai’s presence could act as a magnet for other elite players, raising fears of talent poaching.

“There’s a real risk of player poaching,” another warned.

While Luai gives emphasis to legacy and community impact, critics remain skeptical, pointing to the size and structure of the reported deal. The Wests Tigers, who had built long-term plans around him, now face uncertainty, with his departure seen as a major setback.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Luai deal sparks concern
item.description: THE signing of Samoan international and Wests Tigers five eighth Jarome Luai as PNG Chiefs player No.1 is being hailed as historic, but serious concerns are emerging about its impact on the National Rugby League (NRL). While the move is expected to boost the 19th franchise’s profile, critics warn the deal could disrupt competition balance and trigger a new wave of player movement.
item.content:encoded:
THE signing of Samoan international and Wests Tigers five eighth Jarome Luai as PNG Chiefs player No.1 is being hailed as historic, but serious concerns are emerging about its impact on the National Rugby League (NRL).

While the move is expected to boost the 19th franchise’s profile, critics warn the deal could disrupt competition balance and trigger a new wave of player movement.

Financial player-market structures are believed to be attached to Luai’s deal. Industry figures argue the Chiefs may enjoy a built in advantage, with reports suggesting players could earn more through tax free arrangements compared to standard NRL contracts. Rival clubs fear they cannot compete on equal terms.

“This changes the market,” a club insider said. “If players can earn significantly more for the same salary, it becomes very hard to keep them.”

Luai’s signing is also seen as the possible start of a wider recruitment push. Strong indications suggest he could attract other players to join the Chiefs over the next 12 months, effectively acting as a high profile recruiter. That prospect is already raising alarm, with clubs concerned the new franchise could quickly become a talent magnet, pulling elite players away and weakening established teams.

“There’s a real risk of player poaching,” another source said. “If this becomes a trend, some clubs could lose multiple stars.”

While Luai has spoken publicly about helping grow the game and inspire communities, critics believe the move is largely financially driven.

The size and structure of the reported deal has led to questions about whether money rather than legacy is the key factor.

That perception could influence other players considering similar moves.

The effects are already being felt at club level, with the Wests Tigers facing uncertainty around their long term plans after building around the star playmaker.

His early exit has been described as a major setback, particularly for a club still in a rebuilding phase.

The situation has also sparked debate about fairness among future NRL expansion teams. Rival bids, including those linked to Perth, are not expected to receive the same financial or structural advantages.

There are also concerns about placing too much responsibility on one player.

As the likely “face” of the Chiefs, Luai will carry the pressure of leading on field performance, attracting new signings, and representing the club off the field.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pathway to better players, citizens
item.description: CENTRAL Dabaris chief executive officer Hubert Warupi says the Hiri West Rugby League clinic is helping build both better players and better citizens. Warupi thanked Cathy Neap and the NRL in PNG League Bilong Life team for delivering what he described as a “very important programme” for young children.
item.content:encoded:
CENTRAL Dabaris chief executive officer Hubert Warupi says the Hiri West Rugby League clinic is helping build both better players and better citizens.

Warupi thanked Cathy Neap and the NRL in PNG League Bilong Life team for delivering what he described as a “very important programme” for young children.

The clinic focused on boys and girls aged between six and 12, teaching them the basic skills of rugby league.

Warupi said this age group is often overlooked in development programmes across the country.

“I think this is an area that has been lacking in rugby league in PNG, especially in teaching fundamental skills,” he said.

“It’s not only about what they do on the field, but also what they learn off the field, like sportsmanship and respect. That helps shape their mindset to become better Papua New Guineans.”

He described the partnership between Central Dabaris and the League Bilong Life programme as a holistic approach to development, combining sport with education and personal growth.

Warupi also highlighted the importance of the clinic in supporting the growing Hiri West Schools Rugby League competition, which is now in its second year.

“Last year we had seven schools taking part, and this year four schools are continuing. This competition is a feeder pathway into Central Dabaris, so our focus is clearly on development,” he said.

He added that the presence of Central Dabaris players, including team captain Heni Rawa and other local talents from Papa, Lealea, Boera and Porebada, gave young participants strong role models to look up to.

“We have strong influences here, and we believe we can identify and grow talent from this area,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Co-captain to remain at Wests Tigers for co-captain
item.description: THE Wests Tigers have confirmed that Jarome Luai has activated a clause in his contract, keeping the representative five-eighth at the club for the 2027 NRL season. Luai, who signed with the Wests Tigers ahead of the 2025 NRL season, has also advised of his intention to pursue an opportunity with the PNG Chiefs from 2028 onwards.
item.content:encoded:
THE Wests Tigers have confirmed that Jarome Luai has activated a clause in his contract, keeping the representative five-eighth at the club for the 2027 NRL season.

Luai, who signed with the Wests Tigers ahead of the 2025 NRL season, has also advised of his intention to pursue an opportunity with the PNG Chiefs from 2028 onwards.

The Samoan international has left an indelible mark on the Wests Tigers over the past two seasons, playing a critical role in the club’s rise since his arrival.

The club is looking forward to the impact Luai can have both on and off the field across the remainder of his time at Concord.

Wests Tigers interim chief executive officer Shaun Mielekamp commended Luai for both his professionalism and character.

“I’ve really got to credit Jarome for how he’s handled this process, the way he’s communicated with the club and, ultimately, come to this decision,” Mielekamp said.

“He’s been exceptional since his arrival, which we’ve all witnessed over the past two years, with the quality of his character shining through in a challenging set of circumstances to navigate.

“We’re pleased to have Jarome with us for 2027 and wish him and his family all the best in their eventual next chapter.

“He’s an important figure at our club and will be held in high regard for many years to come.

“For the meantime, we’re all looking forward to the next 18 months and what we can collectively achieve.”

Wests Tigers NRL head coach Benji Marshall said Luai would have a significant role to play over the remainder of his time with the club.

“Jarome has been really important in the direction we’ve taken as a group over the past two seasons,” Marshall said.

“He’s still with us for the next 18 months and the role he plays in that journey continues. We’re all looking forward to having him with us during that time.

“Jarome’s a quality human being who is widely respected across our club. I know how difficult this has been for him, but he’s put his family first and we respect his decision.

“I just want to wish Jarome and his family all the best in what’s ahead for them and thank him for the contribution he’s already made to the Wests Tigers,” Marshall said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Luai signing a massive hit
item.description: THIS is the moment Papua New Guinea rugby league has been waiting for. Jarome Luai signing on to the PNG Chiefs as Player 001 is official, and it’s massive.
item.content:encoded:
THIS is the moment Papua New Guinea rugby league has been waiting for.

Jarome Luai signing on to the PNG Chiefs as Player 001 is official, and it’s massive.

Everyone saw it coming after the Port Moresby visit, but seeing it confirmed still hits different.

You could feel the connection during that weekend trip.

The photos, the crowds, the way he embraced the culture, that wasn’t just a public relations stop.

That was a player understanding exactly what this club means to 10-plus million persons.

Rugby league isn’t a sport here, it’s identity.

And now we’ve got a four-time premiership winner, Origin star, and Samoan captain choosing to be the face of it.

For the Chiefs, this is the perfect first domino.

You don’t build a new franchise with potential, you build it with proven winners.

Luai brings X-factor on the field, but more importantly he brings aura.

Aussie media is already saying other marquee players will follow, and they’re right.

Halves of his calibre attract forwards who want to play off him, backs who want his service, and young players who want to learn from him.

2028 just became a whole lot more real for rival clubs.

You’ve also got to respect how this played out.

He fronted the Wests Tigers, made his decision, and now they’ll have their say.

That’s leadership, and that’s exactly what a brand-new club needs in the locker room from day one.

You’re carrying the hopes of every kid running around barefoot with a footy in every province.

You’re showing the Pacific that this team is for us, by us.

Player 001 will be stitched into history forever. Three years with a 2030 option gives us a real window to build something special.

Thank you to Jarome for believing in the vision.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Angau doing a commendable job
item.description: THERE are posts on Facebook by a certain person on Angau Memorial General Hospital doctors and management. This person might recently have lost a family member at Angau Hospital.
item.content:encoded:
THERE are posts on Facebook by a certain person on Angau Memorial General Hospital doctors and management.

This person might recently have lost a family member at Angau Hospital.

He tries to imply that only at Angau Hospital are patients dying every day.

Does he know that patients are dying every day at every hospital in Papua New Guinea?

The deputy prime minister and the health minister must not stoop so low to give credibility to unsubstantiated claims by this person who has no statistical evidence on deaths every day in all hospitals in PNG to compare with deaths at Angau Hospital.

The biggest killer today is lifestyle disease.

We chew betel nut (buai), smoke, consume alcohol, eat lambs flaps and fast food.

We cannot always blame the health system for our own failures to live a healthy lifestyle.

I was a patient at Angau and recovered fully, and have respect for health staff.

The solution is to make Angau Hospital a standalone like Port Moresby General Hospital.

The Morobe Health Authority (PHA) is the biggest PHA in the country, and the limited resources allocated cannot adequately resource the health services at Angau and the 10 districts of Morobe .

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Greater Sepik unity dream
item.description: FOR generations, “Sepik” has represented more than a river or boundary – it embodies a shared soul. East and West Sepik have been connected through trade, tradition, and history.
item.content:encoded:
FOR generations, “Sepik” has represented more than a river or boundary – it embodies a shared soul.

East and West Sepik have been connected through trade, tradition, and history.

Yet the vision of a “Greater Sepik” remains incomplete if one province feels alienated.

Many West Sepik residents, particularly from Nuku and Aitape-Lumi, face harassment and crime when travelling to Wewak, a vital hub for commerce and services.

When a mother or student fears for her safety, regional identity fractures.

True cohesion requires protection, respect, and inclusion.

This is an urgent call to Governor Allan Bird of East Sepik and Governor Tony Wouwou of West Sepik.

The people need a unified strategy that ensures safety for travellers, equitable economic integration, and political solidarity in national forums.

Unity is not just a word – it is action.

By safeguarding West Sepik residents, strengthening shared markets, and fostering mutual respect, the “Greater Sepik” dream can become reality.

Let the two Sepiks stand together, side by side, as the giants of the north, returning to the bonds that predate provincial lines.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Suspicious structures and appointments
item.description: A GOVERNMENT department has just completed its recruitment exercise. The results of that exercise caused complaints across the department on the practices used in selecting staff and appointing them to positions.
item.content:encoded:
A GOVERNMENT department has just completed its recruitment exercise.

The results of that exercise caused complaints across the department on the practices used in selecting staff and appointing them to positions.

People belonging to one ethnical group seem to be running the whole affairs of this department

A close relative of the secretary was hired by the department at a huge cost to do the structure.

The structure that he did failed to address the core functions of the department and the activities are cut and paste, hence it does not reflect the functions.

Nearly all sections in the department have the same job descriptions for their positions – causing confusion and work duplication.

The person who did the structure for this department has now been appointed to a senior position in this department.

In fact, he was one of those on the selection committee that did all the appointments.

Quite a number of close relatives and cronies of the secretary have been offered the jobs in this department.

The positions they are appointed to control the key areas in the department such as finance and account, internal audit and corporate services wing.

Their appointments to those positions have displaced long-serving staff and made them become unattached, meaning they are without positions.

The selection committee chaired by this secretary had also failed to apply sub-section (5) under section 33 of the Public Service (Management) Act on those positions not affected in the review of the structure.

In place of the substantive occupants of those positions, a number of close relatives of this secretary were brought in from outside the public service through the short-term contract process and engaged against those positions.

That displaced all the substantive occupants of those positions and made them become unattached.

I, therefore, call on the chief secretary to intervene and advise the secretary for Department of Personnel Management to take immediate action as follows:

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific needs disaster displacement included in treaty
item.description: When disasters strike, so does displacement. Each year across the Pacific, cyclones, flooding, coastal erosion and storm surges prompt people to leave their homes.
item.content:encoded:
When disasters strike, so does displacement.

Each year across the Pacific, cyclones, flooding, coastal erosion and storm surges prompt people to leave their homes.

Often this movement is temporary, as people seek shelter in evacuation centres or on higher ground, returning home after the immediate danger has passed.

But sometimes it becomes prolonged or even permanent.

For many Pacific communities, displacement is not an exceptional outcome of disasters; it is a recurring and defining feature of how they are experienced.

Although Pacific countries are on the frontline of the climate crisis, they are also at the forefront of solutions.

The Pacific Regional Framework on Climate Mobility, endorsed by Pacific Leaders in 2023, is a world-leading document that seeks to ensure that the region is resilient and “future-ready” by anticipating, preparing for and responding to the adverse impacts of climate change and disasters, including through mobility.

The region has a new opportunity to show global leadership in this area.

Between now and early 2027, states are negotiating what is likely to become the first-ever global treaty on the Protection of Persons in the Event of Disasters (PPED treaty).

This presents a significant opening for Pacific states to ensure that displacement is reflected in the treaty text.

Despite how central displacement is to the lived reality of disasters, it remains only marginally reflected in the current draft treaty text.

The proposed treaty aims to fill a longstanding gap in international law by establishing a comprehensive legal framework to strengthen the protection of people affected by disasters, including through greater international cooperation and disaster risk reduction (DRR).

The negotiation process is now entering a critical stage. States were invited to submit written proposals for amendments to the draft text by the end of last year.

These proposals were discussed during a session of a dedicated United Nations (UN) Working Group early this month.

These discussions will inform a consolidated draft expected by September 2026, with a view to adoption at a diplomatic conference scheduled for early next year in the Philippines.

And yet, within this process, one issue stands out for its relative absence: disaster-related displacement.

This omission of displacement is striking given its scale and significance globally.

Disasters displace two persons every second.

In 2024 alone, an estimated 65.8 million internal displacements were recorded, with almost 70 per cent triggered by disasters rather than conflict.

Although the current definition of “disaster” (in draft article 3) mentions “mass displacement” as a possible defining characteristic of a disaster, it is otherwise missing from the treaty – and some proposals would remove even this reference.

The silence on displacement in the PPED treaty is not merely a technical oversight.

It risks sidelining one of the most common and significant consequences of disasters at precisely the moment when states are crafting the legal framework intended to address them.

If left unaddressed, the treaty may fall short of capturing how disasters are actually experienced by millions of people worldwide.

For the Pacific, this gap is particularly consequential.

Disaster-related displacement is a defining feature of the region’s security.

Pacific Island countries are among the most exposed in the world to cyclones, flooding, droughts and sea-level rise.

In some cases, entire communities face the prospect of displacement, which is why a number of Pacific states have developed national policies addressing internal displacement, as well as planned relocation guidelines and operating procedures.

Meanwhile, the Falepili Union between Tuvalu and Australia creates a special human mobility pathway which enables up to 280 Tuvaluans annually to migrate to Australia.

This leadership would be welcome in the PPED negotiations.

Pacific states have not yet made any formal proposals on the treaty text, and representation from the region in the Working Group discussions has also been limited.

item.title: Editha on track for art and design career
item.description: EDITHA Tubal has always loved arts and design since she was young, and yesterday, she graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in that very field – arts and design. “Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”
item.content:encoded:
EDITHA Tubal has always loved arts and design since she was young, and yesterday, she graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in that very field – arts and design.

“Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”

She scored a GPA of 4.33 to top her class at the University of PNG School of Humanities and Social Sciences. She described that achievement as “humbling”, after years of sleepless nights and struggling to achieve her goals.

Editha, 32, is the second last-born in a family of five. Her parents, Akuila Tabal and Fidelma Tubal, are from the villages of Taranga in Gazelle district, and Karavi in Kokopo, East New Britain.

Her mother Fidelma is a retired businesswoman. Her late father Akuila was a former public servant in East New Britain.

“I would like to dedicate (my degree) to both my parents, my mum and my late dad.”

She credits her mother as being the driving force behind her success after her dad passed away. She ensured that Editha was well and focused on her studies.

“My biggest supporter was my mum. She called me every day to check on how things were. That enabled me to come up with good grades.”

Her family including her mother, who flew in from East New Britain to support her on her big day, were at the Sir John Guise stadium yesterday to witness her graduation and to congratulate her.

Editha never doubted the path she was going to take regarding her career as she knew that art was what she wanted to pursue.

Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”

From her early passion about art and design, Editha knew that her strength lay in her imaginative and creative traits. They helped her in “deciding things and putting them down on paper”.

Her biggest challenge at university were the nights when she would stay up late to finish her projects.

“The biggest challenge (for me) was probably the sleepless nights during the last few projects.”

Never giving up and always showing up in class helped her achieve her goal.

“I just made sure that I showed up every day. That’s half the work done. I showed up every day and I tried to do the best I could.”

Her support system are her friends, family and, of course, God.

Her word of advice to those still in school pursuing the same programme in the same field of art and design as her is to keep at it.

“It is very enjoyable. They can learn a lot from it when they are taking it.”

A career in art and design often focuses on the blend of creativity, practice, and the necessity of persistence.

Pablo Picasso, one of the most influential artists of the 20th century, when asked about art and design, wrote: “Inspiration exists, but it must find you working,”

And for Editha, earning the top award in her class means the world to her.

“It means a lot to me. I am very happy and humbled. And it makes me want to pursue a career in this field more.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Sex workers speak out about HIV in Papua New Guinea
item.description: Sex workers and their clients can often be seen in Papua New Guinea’s capital Port Moresby. Sylvia Pok, not her real name, is a mother who turned to sex work more than 10 years ago to make ends meet after her marriage broke down.
item.content:encoded:
Sex workers and their clients can often be seen in Papua New Guinea’s capital Port Moresby.

Sylvia Pok, not her real name, is a mother who turned to sex work more than 10 years ago to make ends meet after her marriage broke down.

“A lot of the time we are attacked and almost killed by these men,” Pok told the ABC .

She said it was hard and difficult work, but if she made K200-K300 from a client, the equivalent of US$60-US$95, she could pay her rent and feed herself.

“Even some widow mothers are now hanging around here and we look for money to fill our stomach,” she said.

But physical violence is not the only risk these workers face as PNG battles a surging HIV infection rate.

“There are men who are infected and hanging around these places too, and some come to just harm and kill,” she said.

In June last year, PNG declared an HIV crisis after 11,000 new infections were detected in 2024.

PNG health authorities estimated there was about 30 new HIV infections every day, including seven babies born with the virus.

Official government figures show that today there’s more than 120,000 persons living with HIV in the country, although the real number is thought to be much higher due to gaps in testing.

UNAIDS says sex workers, men who have sex with men and transgender persons are among the groups most vulnerable to HIV.

The latest data from the Health Department shows there’s at least 195,000 female sex workers and 358,000 male clients in the country.

Male sex workers, transgender sex workers and female clients are not separately counted.

Sylvia said clients often offered large sums of money for unprotected sex.

“If he refuses to be safe by using a condom then I will not have sex with him because my life is also important, and I am doing this to live,” she said.

Treena, not her real name, is a transgender woman who is a sex worker.

She’s been in and out of sex work for over 20 years.

“When my mother died in 2002 I just left school and started going into sex work just to survive,” Treena said.

She admits to having unprotected sex with men.

“If they give me a big amount of money, or buy me things, I just go for it, take the risk,”  she said.

The Health Ministry said that there was roughly 5,100 trans women across the country last year.

However, trans women have largely not spoken out about the challenge of combating HIV.

“This is not a good time that we’re going through,” Treena said.

Sylvia and Treena both said advocacy groups and healthcare providers visited sex workers on the street to test them for HIV. But the challenge facing authorities has been decades in the making.

During the 1990s, HIV cases rose steeply in PNG.

By the early 2000s, HIV became the leading cause of death at Port Moresby General Hospital.

Increased testing and availability of medicine in the 2010s saw HIV cases steady, but years of neglect and underfunding has led to one of the region’s worst health crises.

Sex workers, advocates and health workers all agree that the toughest challenge for persons living with HIV is still stigma and discrimination.

For the transgender community, visiting clinics can be daunting.

“Sometimes they’re not really friendly, but those that are sensitised they treat us well,” Treena said.
